name: Stibnite
short_description: BLM public rockhounding near the historic Stibnite mining district. Hunt for quartz, chalcedony, and jasper specimens in gentle rolling terrain at 6600+ feet elevation.
description:
Stibnite is located in the scenic Valley County area of central Idaho, near the site of historic antimony mining operations that shaped this region's geological significance. The area sits within the Idaho batholith province, where mineralized quartz veins and silica-rich deposits were created during Cretaceous granitic intrusions and associated hydrothermal activity. Collectors visit this BLM-managed public land to search for quartz specimens, chalcedony nodules, and jasper pieces that weather out of hillsides and can be found in creek washes.

The terrain here features gentle slopes and moderate elevation around 6600 feet, making it accessible for rockhounders of varying skill levels. The landscape is typical of central Idaho's mixed conifer forest transitioning to more open slopes, with good visibility for spotting specimens. Most productive collecting occurs along old mining roads, hillside exposures, and wash areas where erosion has revealed fresh material.

This is a personal-use rockhounding site on BLM land with no permit requirements, making it an excellent choice for casual collectors and families looking to try their luck without bureaucratic barriers. The 25 lb per day limit means you can take home a meaningful collection while respecting regulations. The area's mining history adds educational value to any visit, and you may encounter remnants of old workings or equipment.
history: Stibnite was the site of significant antimony mining operations during the early to mid-20th century, particularly during World War II when antimony was strategically important for military alloys and ammunition. The mineralization here resulted from Cretaceous-age granitic intrusions that generated extensive hydrothermal systems, depositing quartz veins, silica-rich fluids, and associated metallic minerals throughout the region.
tips: Search along the hillsides above old mining roads and in creek washes where material naturally concentrates - look for white to gray quartz chunks and banded chalcedony nodules ranging from 1-4 inches in size. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and sturdy digging bar to work loose material from weathered outcrops; the rocky terrain here responds well to moderate prying. Focus your search where the slope steepens slightly, as water erosion naturally concentrates heavier specimens. Early morning visits help avoid afternoon weather shifts at this elevation.
terrain_notes: Elevation 6631 feet with gentle to moderate slopes across rolling terrain. Mixed conifer forest with open hillside exposures. Walking distances from parking areas typically 0.5 to 2 miles depending on access roads. Rocky, uneven footing with some loose material; sturdy boots strongly recommended. Vegetation is scattered to moderate density.
best_season: June - September. High elevation and winter snowpack make access difficult from October through May. Summer months offer dry conditions, clear skies, and established road access.
written_directions: From McCall (approximately 20 miles south), take ID-55 north through the Valley County area. Follow signs toward the Stibnite mining district area. Access the site via Forest Road 501 (check current BLM road conditions before departing). GPS coordinates 44.897873, -115.338123 mark the general collecting area. High-clearance vehicles recommended for final approach roads; verify road passability with Valley County BLM field office before traveling.
road_conditions: Rough forest road, potential washboard. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Road condition varies seasonally; check BLM website before visiting.
parking_notes: Informal pullout areas along forest roads near collecting sites. No developed parking facility. Space for 2-4 vehicles.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Collect only loose specimens and weathered material; do not blast or heavily modify outcrops. Pack out all trash.
rules:

nearest_services: McCall, Idaho (approximately 20 miles south). Full services including gas, food, water, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Gentle to moderate slopes with uneven rocky terrain. No wheelchair access beyond parking areas. Sturdy footwear and moderate fitness required. Most collecting areas accessible to fit adults and older children. High elevation may affect some visitors; allow time to acclimate.

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding near Stibnite, Idaho?
faq.answer: The primary specimens found at Stibnite are quartz, chalcedony, and jasper. Quartz pieces range from small fragments to hand-sized specimens with good clarity. Chalcedony nodules often display banding and color variation, while jasper can be found in various earth tones. Most specimens weather naturally from hillsides and creek washes.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Stibnite on BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding at Stibnite. As BLM public land in Idaho, you have free access for personal use collection. You simply need to follow the 25 lb per day limit and no-commercial-use rules established by the Bureau of Land Management.

faq.question: Is Stibnite rockhounding site beginner-friendly?
faq.answer: Yes, Stibnite is excellent for beginners. The gentle terrain, easy specimen identification, and lack of permit requirements make it ideal for first-time rockhounders and families. Most productive collecting occurs along open hillsides and washes where material is easily spotted without technical excavation skills.

faq.question: When is the best season for rockhounding in the Stibnite area?
faq.answer: June through September offers the best conditions for collecting at Stibnite. The high elevation (6600+ feet) brings heavy snow in winter months, closing access roads. Summer provides dry conditions, established road access, and comfortable collecting weather without afternoon thunderstorms common in shoulder seasons.

faq.question: What tools should I bring for rockhounding near McCall at Stibnite?
faq.answer: Bring a digging bar or pry bar to work material from weathered outcrops, a 5-gallon bucket for transport, gloves, sturdy boots with good traction, a backpack for water and supplies, and a GPS device or phone with downloaded maps. High-clearance vehicle is recommended for forest road access.

faq.question: How far from McCall is the Stibnite rockhounding area?
faq.answer: Stibnite is located approximately 20 miles north of McCall, Idaho, in the Valley County area. Access requires driving ID-55 north and then taking Forest Road 501 into the mining district area. Plan for 45 minutes to 1 hour drive time from McCall, plus additional time for field collection.
